title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Opportunities
meta_description: Learn how to use intuition when evaluating opportunities. Stop overthinking and start trusting your gut to make clear, confident decisions.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Opportunities
og_description: Learn how to use intuition when evaluating opportunities. Stop overthinking and start trusting your gut to make clear, confident decisions.
body:
You've Got an Offer. Now What?

You're sitting there, staring at an email, a text, or maybe just a thought that won't leave you alone. It's an opportunity - a job, a side project, a relationship, a move to a new city. Your logical brain is firing off all the pros and cons. You've got a spreadsheet going. You've asked three friends for their opinion. And you're still stuck.

That's because you're trying to solve a question that can't be answered with a calculator. Opportunities aren't math problems. They're energy exchanges. And your intuition is the only tool that can read the room - or in this case, read the future you're walking into.

Let me be clear about something right off the bat: I'm not saying throw your brain out the window. I'm saying your brain is one player on a team, and right now you've benched the star player. Your intuition is that star player. It's the part of you that knows before you know. It's the gut feeling that tightens when something's off, or the lightness that spreads through your chest when something's right. You've felt it. You just haven't trusted it.

So let's fix that. Here's how you actually use your intuition when evaluating opportunities - without losing your mind or your rent money.

Why Your Brain Lies to You About Opportunity

Your brain is a survival machine. It's not designed to help you thrive. It's designed to keep you safe. And safe means staying put, not taking risks, and ignoring the whisper that says "this could be everything." Your brain will hand you a list of reasons why you shouldn't take the leap. It'll tell you you're not ready, not qualified, not lucky enough. It'll point to every failure you've ever had and say "see? Told you."

But here's the thing your brain won't tell you: it's terrified of the unknown. And opportunity is the unknown wearing a fancy outfit. So when you try to evaluate an opportunity purely with logic, you're asking a scared guard dog to give you directions through a dark forest. It can't. It'll just bark at shadows.

Your intuition is different. It's not scared of the dark. It's been in the dark before. It knows the terrain. It's the part of you that's connected to something bigger than your fear - your soul, your purpose, the thread of your life that's been pulling you forward since the day you were born. That part doesn't need a spreadsheet. It needs you to shut up and listen.

The opportunity that's right for you won't feel like a gamble you're taking. It'll feel like a truth you're finally admitting to yourself.

Does that land? Because I've seen people turn down the perfect thing because it didn't look perfect on paper. And I've seen people jump into disasters because the logic looked good. The difference was always in how it felt in their body, not how it looked in their mind.

The First Step: Get Out of Your Head and Into Your Body

Before you can evaluate any opportunity, you have to get grounded. I don't mean "meditate for an hour" (though if that's your thing, go for it). I mean you need to drop out of the mental chatter and into your physical sensations. Your intuition doesn't speak in words. It speaks in sensations - tightness, openness, heat, cold, expansion, contraction.

So here's what you do. Take the opportunity - the job offer, the relationship, the move - and hold it in your mind. Don't analyze it. Just picture it. See yourself saying yes. See yourself in that new role, that new city, that new dynamic. Now scan your body from head to toe. Where do you feel something?

Most people feel it in their chest or their gut. A tight chest usually means fear. A knotted stomach usually means "no." But here's the trick: you have to learn the difference between fear and a "no." Fear feels sharp, fast, and reactive. A "no" feels heavy, dull, and sinking. Fear says "I might fail." A "no" says "this isn't mine."

Try it right now with something small. Think about eating a food you hate. Notice how your body reacts. That's a "no." Now think about a food you love. That lightness, that openness? That's a "yes." Your body knows. It's been telling you all along. You just haven't been listening.

If you want to get better at this, I recommend picking up a copy of Daring Greatly by Brené Brown. It's not a psychic development book, but it'll help you understand why your fear of vulnerability is blocking your intuition. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Second Step: Ask the Right Questions

Your brain will ask: "How much does it pay?" "What's the commute?" "What will people think?" These are not intuition questions. These are safety questions dressed up as practicality. Your intuition needs different questions. Questions that open doors instead of closing them.

Here are the questions I want you to ask yourself when you're evaluating an opportunity:

  • Does this opportunity expand me or shrink me?
  • When I imagine saying yes, does my energy rise or drop?
  • Who will I become if I take this path? Do I like that person?
  • What's the feeling behind the fear - is it "I'm not ready" or is it "this isn't right"?
  • If I knew I couldn't fail, would I still want this?

The last one is key. Because fear of failure is real, but it's also the most common disguise for a "no." If you take failure off the table and you still feel a "no," that's your intuition screaming. Listen to it.

I had a client once who was offered a promotion that looked amazing on paper. More money, more status, more responsibility. But every time she pictured herself in that role, her chest tightened and her stomach dropped. She thought it was nerves. It wasn't. It was a "no." She turned it down, took a lateral move instead, and within six months she was running a department she actually loved. The promotion would have crushed her. Her intuition knew it. Her brain was just late to the party.

The Third Step: Time Travel (No, Really)

Your intuition doesn't live in the present moment the way your brain does. It's not bound by time. It can sense the future because the future is already vibrating in the present. You just have to tune into it.

Here's a technique I use all the time. Close your eyes and imagine yourself one year after saying yes to this opportunity. Walk through a typical day. What do you feel? Are you excited? Bored? Resentful? Peaceful? Now imagine yourself one year after saying no. Walk through that day. What do you feel? Regret? Relief? Freedom?

Your body will tell you the truth. If saying yes leads to a body that feels heavy, closed, or dull, that's your answer. If saying no leads to a body that feels light, open, or expansive, that's also your answer. The future self doesn't lie. It's not trying to protect you from risk. It's showing you the energetic consequence of your choice.

This is where a lot of people get stuck because they can't quiet their mind enough to feel the future. If that's you, try writing it down. Journal about both futures. Don't think. Just write. Let the words come. You'll be surprised at what shows up on the page. Your intuition will write through you if you let it.

For more on this kind of time-travel intuition work, check out The Gifts of Imperfection by Brené Brown. It's a short read but it'll help you stop performing for other people and start living for your own truth. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Fourth Step: Check for Energetic Alignment

This is the step most people skip because it feels "woo-woo." But here's the deal: everything is energy. The opportunity, you, the people involved, the timing. They all have a frequency. When frequencies match, things flow. When they don't, you'll feel friction.

Energetic alignment isn't about whether the opportunity is "good" or "bad." It's about whether it's good for you. A dream job for your best friend could be a nightmare for you. A relationship that looks perfect from the outside could drain your soul. You have to check the energy, not the appearance.

Here's how you do it. Sit quietly. Bring the opportunity into your awareness. Now ask yourself: "Does this feel like a door that's open, or a door I have to push open?" An open door feels light, easy, and welcoming. A door you have to push open feels heavy, forced, and resistant. If you're pushing, you're probably in the wrong place.

I know this sounds simple, but it's deep. Most of us spend our lives pushing on doors that were never meant to open. We think struggle equals growth. Sometimes it does. But sometimes struggle is just a sign that you're fighting your own truth. Your intuition can tell the difference. Your brain can't.

If you want to go deeper into energetic alignment, I recommend Rising Strong by Brené Brown. It's about what happens after you fall - and trust me, you will fall. But the fall is easier when you're aligned with your truth. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Fifth Step: The 24-Hour Rule

Here's a practical tool. When you're evaluating an opportunity, give yourself 24 hours of no decision-making. During that time, don't analyze, don't ask for advice, don't make lists. Just sit with the opportunity. Let it breathe. Let it sit in your energy field.

Notice what happens. Does the opportunity feel different on day two than it did on day one? Does the excitement fade or grow? Does the fear settle or intensify? Your intuition needs time to speak. It's not a text message. It's a letter that arrives in pieces.

During those 24 hours, pay attention to synchronicities. Do you keep hearing about this opportunity? Do you see signs that point toward or away from it? Does someone randomly mention something that confirms your gut feeling? The universe talks to you through coincidence. But it's only coincidence until you start paying attention. Then it's guidance.

I had a student who was torn between two job offers. She did the 24-hour rule. During that time, she saw a license plate that said "YES" on one side of the road and a car with a flat tire on the other. She laughed, but she also listened. The "yes" offer felt right. The "no" offer felt like the flat tire. She took the "yes." Best decision of her life.

Does that land? It sounds crazy when you say it out loud. But your intuition works through symbols and signs. You just have to be awake enough to see them.

The Sixth Step: Trust the "No" as Much as the "Yes"

This is where most people fall apart. They can feel the "yes" - it's exciting, it's expansive, it's full of possibility. But the "no" feels like rejection, like fear, like failure. So they ignore it. They rationalize it. They tell themselves they're just being scared.

But a "no" is just as valuable as a "yes." Sometimes more. A "no" protects you. A "no" keeps you on your path. A "no" is your intuition saying "not this, because there's something better." And if you don't honor the "no," you'll end up in a place that drains you, dims you, and makes you forget who you are.

I've seen it happen a hundred times. Someone takes the job because the money is good, even though their gut said no. Six months later, they're miserable, burnt out, and wondering where they went wrong. The money wasn't worth it. It never is.

Trust the "no." It's not a closed door. It's a redirection. And redirections are how you find your actual path.

For more on trusting the "no" and the messy middle of decision-making, read Braving the Wilderness by Brené Brown. It's about standing alone in your truth - even when it's hard. See on Amazon (paid link)

The Seventh Step: Act Before You're Ready

Here's the paradox of intuition: it will never give you 100% certainty. If you wait for certainty, you'll wait forever. Intuition gives you a nudge, a whisper, a feeling. Then it asks you to move. It's not a guarantee. It's a direction.

So when you've done the body scan, asked the questions, time-traveled, checked the energy, and waited 24 hours, and you still feel a pull - act. Don't wait until you're ready. You'll never be ready. Act on the 70% certainty. The other 30% is where faith and courage come in.

I'm not saying be reckless. I'm saying don't let perfect be the enemy of aligned. If your intuition is pointing you toward something, take a step. It doesn't have to be a leap. It can be a small step. A conversation. An application. A "yes" to a first date. A deposit on a flight. The step itself will give you more information than sitting still ever could.

Your intuition is a muscle. It grows stronger with use. Every time you act on it, you build trust. And trust is what turns intuition from a vague feeling into a reliable guide.

When Your Intuition Conflicts with Logic

This will happen. Probably a lot. Your brain will give you a spreadsheet full of reasons to say no, and your gut will be screaming yes. Or vice versa. So what do you do?

You don't pick one over the other. You integrate them. You use logic to plan the logistics, and intuition to set the direction. Logic is the vehicle. Intuition is the driver. You need both. But the driver decides where you're going.

If your intuition says yes and your logic says no, ask your logic: "What is the fear here?" Often, the logic is just fear in a suit and tie. If your intuition says no and your logic says yes, ask your intuition: "What am I not seeing?" Sometimes the intuition is protecting you from something your brain hasn't caught yet.

The goal isn't silence the brain. The goal is to let the intuition lead, with the brain as a support system. Your brain is great at details. It's terrible at destiny. Let it handle the details. Let your soul handle the direction.

The Bottom Line

You already know the answer. You've always known it. The opportunity in front of you - you know if it's right. You can feel it. But you're afraid to trust it because you've been wrong before. We all have. Being wrong about an opportunity doesn't mean your intuition is broken. It means you were learning to hear it more clearly.

Your intuition is not a magic eight ball. It's a relationship. And like any relationship, it takes time, honesty, and practice. Start small. Practice on the little things - what to eat, which route to take, who to call. Build the muscle. Then when the big opportunities come, you'll be ready.

You're not broken. You're not too sensitive. You're not imagining things. You're just waking up to the fact that you have a guidance system that's been whispering to you your whole life. It's time to turn up the volume.

Ready to go deeper? Take our free assessment to see where your intuition is strong and where it needs a little more trust. Take the intuition assessment here.
